title: High Spinal After Neuraxial Block
description: Spinal anesthetic spreads high: progressive sympathectomy with hypotension and bradycardia, dyspnea and arm weakness. Support the airway, give vasopressors, stabilize.
learningObjectives: Recognize the rising level of a high spinal (hypotension, bradycardia, dyspnea, arm weakness)
Explain sympathectomy physiology: SVR, venous return, and cardioaccelerator block
Choose vasopressors that address both tone and heart rate

events.message: Spinal placed at L3-4 with hyperbaric bupivacaine. Patient supine.
Patient: "I feel like I can't take a deep breath... my hands are tingling."
She is pale and anxious. BP and heart rate are falling.

expectedActions.feedback: High-flow oxygen while the level is high.
Help called early — appropriate for a rising block.

dangerousActions.feedback: Prolonged untreated hypotension during a high spinal risks maternal and fetal hypoperfusion.
Desaturation during a high spinal demands immediate oxygenation and ventilation support.

debrief.summary: A rising spinal blocks sympathetic outflow: SVR and venous return fall, and with T1-T4 spread the cardioaccelerators are blocked, so the usual compensatory tachycardia is replaced by bradycardia.
teachingPoints: Sympathectomy drops SVR and preload — pressure falls before the airway is threatened.
Cardioaccelerator (T1-T4) block causes bradycardia in the face of hypotension: treat rate and tone together.
Ephedrine or epinephrine treat both rate and pressure; phenylephrine treats tone but can worsen bradycardia.
Dyspnea and hand weakness signal a high (cervical-approaching) level — prepare airway support early.
